entries.title: Edible fruit and vitamin C
entries.summary: Bell and chili peppers are edible fruits used as foods. Peppers can contribute vitamin C, although the amount varies by cultivar, color, preparation, and serving size.
entries.caution: This is nutrition information, not a recommendation to treat or prevent a medical condition. Hot peppers may irritate some people.

entries.title: No treatment claim
entries.summary: This catalog does not recommend Capsicum as medicine or as a treatment for any condition.
entries.caution: Do not replace medical care or prescribed treatment with a plant or food.

entries.title: Not a pet dietary supplement
entries.summary: There is no reviewed pet-health benefit for feeding bell or hot peppers in this catalog. Pets should receive food formulated for their species.
entries.caution: Avoid spicy, seasoned, salted, or prepared human foods. Contact a veterinarian for feeding or exposure questions.

entries.title: No veterinary treatment claim
entries.summary: This catalog does not recommend Capsicum as a veterinary medicine or treatment.
entries.caution: Do not use peppers or pepper extracts to treat a pet.

sources.name: USDA FoodData Central: Peppers fact sheet

entries.title: Oat food crop
entries.summary: Avena sativa is the common oat, a crop used for human food. A tray sold as cat grass is intended for grazing and is not automatically prepared or labeled as human food.
entries.caution: Use only food that has been grown, handled, and labeled for human consumption.

entries.title: No treatment claim
entries.summary: This catalog does not recommend oat grass or oat products as medicine or as a treatment for any condition.
entries.caution: Do not replace medical care or prescribed treatment with a plant or food.

entries.title: Grazing and enrichment
entries.summary: Oat grass is one of the cereal grasses commonly sold as cat grass and may provide a supervised grazing or enrichment option for some cats. Cats do not need grass when they are eating a balanced diet.
entries.caution: Offer only clean, pesticide-free grass. Remove moldy plants, limit grazing, and ask a veterinarian about vomiting or other symptoms.

entries.title: No veterinary treatment claim
entries.summary: Cat grass is not a medicine and is not a treatment for hairballs, constipation, illness, or poisoning.
entries.caution: Contact a veterinarian if a pet is unwell or eats a potentially harmful plant.

sources.name: VCA Animal Hospitals: Grass treats for cats
Cornell Feline Health Center: Houseplant chewing
